professor.name: Dr. Halvorsen
professor.text: We've been examining how the deep ocean is becoming a frontier for mineral extraction. Companies can now harvest potato-sized nodules rich in cobalt and nickel from the abyssal plain, the very metals our batteries depend on. Some marine scientists argue this is less destructive than expanding land mines, while others warn that the seafloor recovers over millennia, not years. Should we permit commercial deep-sea mining before we fully understand these ecosystems? Why or why not?

students.name: Eric
students.text: I'd cautiously support it, but only under strict monitoring. Our transition to clean energy demands enormous quantities of these metals, and concentrating extraction on relatively barren nodule fields might disturb fewer species than pushing terrestrial mines deeper into rainforests. The environmental cost here is comparative, not absolute.

students.name: Tim
students.text: The fundamental problem is irreversibility. Abyssal ecosystems grow at an almost unimaginably slow pace, so any damage we inflict becomes effectively permanent on human timescales. Until we can predict the consequences with reasonable confidence, authorizing large-scale mining is a reckless gamble with biodiversity we barely understand.

professor.name: Dr. Okonkwo
professor.text: We've been discussing how social platforms and news organizations share revenue. Several countries now require large platforms to pay publishers whenever their articles are linked or previewed in a feed. Supporters say this funds struggling local journalism, while critics argue it effectively taxes the basic act of linking and mostly enriches large media conglomerates. Do you think governments should require platforms to pay news outlets for linked content? Why?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Local journalism is collapsing, and these payments redirect advertising revenue back toward the organizations that actually investigate and report. Platforms have profited for years from content they never produced. A mandatory bargaining framework simply corrects an imbalance that the market, left to itself, has consistently failed to resolve.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: I'm skeptical, because taxing links distorts how the open web fundamentally works. These arrangements tend to reward a handful of dominant publishers while smaller outlets and independent writers see almost nothing. If we genuinely want sustainable journalism, direct public funding or nonprofit models would target the problem far more precisely.

professor.name: Dr. Renteria
professor.text: We've been studying how firms structure working time. A growing number of companies have trialed a four-day week while keeping salaries unchanged, and many report steady output alongside happier, healthier staff. Some economists argue that compressed schedules raise productivity per hour, while others suspect the gains fade once the novelty wears off or simply shift the same workload onto fewer days. Would a shorter workweek benefit most industries, or only a few? Explain your view.

students.name: Diane
students.text: For most knowledge-based work, output depends on focus rather than hours logged, so compressing the week can genuinely raise productivity per hour. Employees return rested and waste less time on low-value meetings. The evidence from recent trials suggests these improvements are structural, not merely a temporary motivational spike.

students.name: Wendy
students.text: I think the benefits are confined to particular sectors. Manufacturing, healthcare, and retail depend on continuous coverage, so a shorter week means either hiring additional staff or cramming output into fewer, more exhausting days. Generalizing results from white-collar pilots to the entire economy strikes me as distinctly premature.

professor.name: Dr. Vance
professor.text: We've been discussing how automated systems increasingly make consequential decisions, from approving loans to screening job applicants. Some computer scientists argue that companies should be legally required to publish the source code behind such systems so independent researchers can audit them for bias. Others counter that disclosure invites manipulation and exposes trade secrets without genuinely improving fairness. Should high-stakes decision algorithms be open to public inspection? Why or why not?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: When an algorithm can deny someone a mortgage or a job, accountability has to outweigh commercial secrecy. Public auditing is the most reliable way to expose discriminatory patterns that companies have little incentive to reveal themselves. Transparency doesn't guarantee fairness, but opacity virtually guarantees that bias goes unchallenged.

students.name: Marco
students.text: Releasing source code rarely reveals what people assume it will. The discrimination usually hides in the training data, not the visible logic, so publication exposes trade secrets while inviting applicants to game the system. I'd favor independent regulatory audits conducted under confidentiality over wholesale disclosure that mainly benefits competitors.
